summary: Test A: rediscover CVE-2021-32790 in WooCommerce 5.5.0 (blind). Test B: review smart-slider-3. Opus 4.8, throttled, adversarial verify.

confirmed.title: Media directory listing AJAX action lacks capability check (only nonce required)
confirmed.auth_required: Authenticated (any logged-in user who can obtain the 'nextend_security' nonce)
confirmed.data_flow: wp_ajax_smart-slider3 -> AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax() -> display_controller('sliders','index',true) -> AbstractApplicationType::processRequest() reads attacker-controlled nextendcontroller/nextendaction from request -> dispatches ControllerAjaxBrowse::actionIndex(). actionIndex() only calls validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce with the static action 'nextend_security') and performs NO current_user_can() check (unlike actionUpload which checks 'upload_files'). The page-render path enforces checkForCap()/unfiltered_html, but the AJAX path (display_admin_ajax) does not. The handler then takes $_REQUEST['path'], builds a directory under the uploads/images folder, and returns directory + file (jpg/png/gif/svg/mp4/...) listings with their URLs. realpath()+strpos($path,$root)===0 mitigate path traversal for non-symlink paths, so the exposure is limited to enumerating the contents/URLs of the media/images folder rather than arbitrary filesystem read.

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated, low-privilege (Contributor/Author with edit_posts or edit_pages, which lets them obtain the nextend_security nonce via the TinyMCE editor integration). No upload_files or admin/unfiltered_html capability needed.
verdict.reasoning:
Verified the full chain. wp_ajax_smart-slider3 -> AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax() (AdminHelper.php:155-158) does NOT call checkForCap(), unlike every page-render handler. It calls display_controller('sliders','index',true) -> AbstractApplicationType::processRequest() which reads attacker-controlled nextendcontroller/nextendaction from Request::getCmd() (AbstractApplicationType.php:66-76). getControllerAjaxBrowse() returns ControllerAjaxBrowse (ApplicationTypeAdmin.php:161-163). actionIndex() (line 17) calls ONLY validateToken() = wp_verify_nonce(nextend_nonce,'nextend_security') (PlatformForm.php:21-22); no current_user_can() — confirmed by contrast with actionUpload() at line 145 which requires upload_files. It then takes $_REQUEST['path'] and returns directory + media-file listings with URLs.

Nonce obtainability for non-admins: HelperTinyMCE::addButton() (admin_init hook) emits wp_create_nonce('nextend_security') via Predefined::backend() for any user with edit_posts OR edit_pages on the post/page editor screens (HelperTinyMCE.php:28-70). Since the nonce action is a static string (not capability-bound) and wp_verify_nonce only validates action+session, a Contributor/Author can generate a valid nonce for their own session and call the Browse action — a genuine privilege gap (they typically lack upload_files).

Defenses that DO apply: realpath()+strpos($path,$root)!==0 (lines 24-26) constrain enumeration to the uploads images folder for non-symlink paths, and the nonce keeps it authenticated. These limit impact to media-directory enumeration (names + URLs), not arbitrary file read — consistent with low severity. No capability-check sanitizer saves the AJAX path; the missing current_user_can() is real. Claim confirmed.

confirmed.title: Case-sensitive javascript: check and incomplete attribute sanitization in custom HTML filter (filter_attributes_on)
confirmed.data_flow: Slide item content (heading/text/button) authored via admin editor is stored, then on the frontend passed through Sanitize::filter_allowed_html() -> filter_attributes_on() in ItemHeadingFrontend.php:48, ItemTextFrontend.php:42/60/86, ItemButtonFrontend.php:34. filter_attributes_on() only removes attributes whose name starts with 'on' and only strips href values that contain the exact lowercase substring 'javascript:' (strpos at line 684 is case-sensitive). A value like href="JaVaScRiPt:alert(document.cookie)" or use of other vectors (e.g. with data:/vbscript:, or other URL-bearing allowed attributes such as / which are never checked) survives the filter and is rendered verbatim on the public frontend, executing in the context of every site visitor.

verdict.auth_required: admin/editor (smartslider_edit capability; by default only administrators, and admin UI also gates on unfiltered_html)
verdict.reasoning: Code confirmed at /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/.claude/worktrees/woo-security-review/plugins/src/smart-slider-3/Nextend/Framework/Sanitize.php:684. The href check uses strpos (case-sensitive), not stripos, so href="JaVaScRiPt:alert(...)" survives filter_attributes_on() and is rendered verbatim on the frontend via Html::tag in ItemHeadingFrontend.php:48, ItemTextFrontend.php:42/60/86, ItemButtonFrontend.php:34. filter_allowed_html() is the ONLY sanitizer on that path (no later wp_kses). The case-sensitive javascript: bypass is therefore a REAL sanitizer-bypass. Caveats lowering impact: (1) The on* attribute removal at line 677 correctly strips onerror/onload etc. for every tag, so the strongest auto-firing vector is closed. (2) The claim's / vector is INCORRECT — javascript: in img/source src does not execute in any modern browser; vbscript: is dead and top-level data: navigation is blocked. So the only genuine survivor is , which requires a victim CLICK (not stored auto-XSS). Threat model / privilege boundary: smartslider_edit is granted by default ONLY to administrator (InstallWordPress.php:10-16) and the admin UI gates on current_user_can('unfiltered_html') (AdminHelper.php:116). On a default single-site install the only actor with access is an administrator, who already has unfiltered_html and can natively inject raw

confirmed.title: Stored XSS via unescaped Vimeo 'start' value concatenated into inline JavaScript
confirmed.auth_required: User able to edit slider/slide layer data (typically admin/editor), or attacker controlling dynamic generator variables substituted by Slide::fill()
confirmed.data_flow: ItemVimeo layer field 'start' (stored slide item value) -> ItemVimeoFrontend::render() reads $this->data->get('start','0') -> passed through $owner->fill() (Slide::fill(), which performs {variable}/{func()} template substitution and does NOT HTML/JS-escape, and can inject generator-sourced/external values) -> concatenated RAW (no json_encode, no quotes, no intval) as the final argument of the addScript() string: new _N2.FrontendItemVimeo(this, "id", "elementID", <toJSON>, <hasImage>, <start>); -> addScript() pushes the string into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks -> AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118/125) does implode($initCallbacks) and embeds it verbatim inside a JS function body: "initCallbacks":function(){ ...<start>... } -> emitted into the frontend page and executed by the browser. A 'start' value such as `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the function-call argument and runs arbitrary JS. Note the sibling YouTube item (ItemYouTubeFrontend.php line 119) handles the identical 'start' value safely by routing it only through json_encode via toJSON(), confirming this raw concatenation is a defect; the Number form-field constraint is client-side only and not enforced server-side at this sink.

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated user with the plugin's `smartslider_edit` capability (admin/editor) plus a valid CSRF token; the injected payload then executes for unauthenticated frontend visitors (stored XSS, not pure self-XSS).
verdict.reasoning:
The full exploit path holds end to end.

SINK (confirmed): ItemVimeoFrontend.php line 102 builds the init script by RAW concatenation of the last argument: `... , ' . $hasImage . ', ' . $owner->fill($this->data->get('start', '0')) . ');'` — no quotes, no json_encode, no intval. This string is pushed via addScript() into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks (AbstractRenderable.php:137), then AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118 implode, line 125) embeds it VERBATIM inside `"initCallbacks":function(){ ... }` which is emitted to the page and executed. A value like `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the call and runs arbitrary JS.

SOURCE / no sanitizer on any path:
- Save path: ControllerAjaxSlides::actionEdit() -> ModelSlides::save() -> convertSlideDataToDatabaseRow() (ModelSlides.php:658) stores `$row['slide'] = $slideData['layers']` (the full layer tree incl. `start`) as json_encode'd JSON with NO per-field sanitization. The `Number` form field (`min => 0`) in ItemVimeo::renderFields() is a client-side-only constraint, never enforced server-side at this sink.
- Slide::fill() (Slide.php:513) returns the value unchanged when no variables, or does {variable}/{func()} template substitution — neither path HTML/JS-escapes, so it cannot save the sink.
- The sink itself applies no escaping.

SIBLING PROOF: ItemYouTubeFrontend.php handles the identical `start` value safely by routing it only through toJSON()/json_encode (line 119, never raw-concatenated), confirming the Vimeo raw concatenation is a real defect, not intended design.

AUTH: actionEdit requires validateToken() (CSRF nonce) and validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). That gates exploitation to a privileged editing user, but the payload is stored and runs on the PUBLIC frontend for all visitors and any other admin viewing the slider — so it is genuine stored XSS reaching lower-privilege victims, not self-XSS. WP core defenses do not help: the value is placed in a JS execution context (not HTML), so wp_kses/esc_html are irrelevant, and the plugin applies none anyway.

Severity medium is correct: real and exploitable, but requires an authenticated slider-editing capability to plant the payload.

all.title: WP_Query query-var injection via unsanitized search keyword (auth info disclosure of non-public posts)
all.vuln_class: WordPress query-var injection / broken access control (information disclosure)
all.auth_required: low-privileged authenticated user (e.g. Subscriber). The handler is registered on add_action('wp_ajax_smart-slider3', ...) in Nextend/SmartSlider3/Platform/WordPress/Admin/AdminHelper.php:35 and dispatched via processRequest() which reads the controller/action from request params; display_admin_ajax() (AdminHelper.php:155) does NOT call checkForCap(), and actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent only call validateToken() (a shared 'nextend_security' nonce) with no validatePermission(). So any logged-in user who can obtain the nonce reaches it.
all.data_flow: Request::$REQUEST->getVar('keyword') (raw, only stripslashed - Nextend/Framework/Request/Parser/WordPressRequestParser.php:19, Storage.php:50) -> ControllerAjaxContent::actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent (ControllerAjaxContent.php:15,22) -> Content::searchLink/searchContent -> WordPressContent::searchLink line 20 / searchContent line 45: new WP_Query('post_type=any&posts_per_page=20&post_status=publish&s=' . $keyword). The string-form WP_Query argument is parsed with wp_parse_args/parse_str, so a keyword containing '&' and '=' injects/overrides arbitrary query vars. e.g. keyword='x&post_status=private,draft,pending' overrides the earlier post_status=publish, causing the query to return non-public posts; their title, excerpt, permalink and attachment image are returned in the AJAX response. Other query vars (e.g. p, page_id, post__in) can likewise be injected to enumerate specific posts.

verdict.auth_required: Effectively requires Smart Slider admin/editor capability (unfiltered_html / edit_posts / smartslider). A Subscriber CANNOT reach the sink because they cannot obtain a valid nextend_security nonce. The claimed Subscriber-level exploit is not achievable.
verdict.reasoning:
The query-var injection mechanism itself is real: WordPressContent::searchLink/searchContent (lines 20/45) use the STRING form new WP_Query('post_type=any&posts_per_page=20&post_status=publish&s=' . $keyword), which WP_Query parses via wp_parse_args/parse_str, so a keyword containing &post_status=private overrides the earlier post_status. The source (Request::getVar('keyword')) is only stripslashed, never sanitized (WordPressRequestParser.php:19). Routing is reachable: processRequest reads nextendcontroller/nextendaction from the request (AbstractApplicationType.php:66-71), so the hardcoded display_admin_ajax -> ('sliders','index') can be redirected to Content/SearchLink, and getControllerAjaxContent() exists (ApplicationTypeAdmin.php:151). The claim is also correct that display_admin_ajax() does NOT call checkForCap() and the actions call only validateToken(), not validatePermission().

What REFUTES the exploit at the stated (Subscriber) privilege level is the nonce gate. ControllerAjaxContent::actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent call $this->validateToken(), which resolves to the AjaxController override (AjaxController.php:29-35) that calls $this->response->error() on nonce failure and TERMINATES (the base AbstractController version merely returns false, but that is not the one used here). validateToken -> Form::checkToken -> wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['nextend_nonce'], 'nextend_security') (PlatformForm.php:22). WordPress nonces are bound to the current user ID + session, so a nonce minted for an admin is invalid under a Subscriber's session.

The nextend_security nonce is emitted ONLY by Predefined::backend() (Predefined.php:31, via Form::tokenizeUrl()) and admin form rendering, all of which run exclusively in admin/editor contexts that require smartslider / edit_posts / unfiltered_html capability (SmartSlider3PlatformWordPress.php:48-62 gates on is_admin()/Gutenberg editor; AbstractAdminController and admin_head paths). Critically, Predefined::frontend() — the version invoked by the front-end controller initialize() (AbstractController.php:125) and public slider rendering — does NOT call tokenizeUrl() and therefore does NOT expose the nonce. There is no front-end or low-privilege code path that mints nextend_security for a Subscriber's own session. Consequently a Subscriber cannot obtain a usable nonce and cannot reach the sink.

Only users who already hold Smart Slider admin/editor capability (unfiltered_html/edit_posts) can pass validateToken. Those users can already read all posts (including private/draft) through normal WP admin, so the post_status override discloses nothing beyond their existing authorization — no privilege boundary is crossed. The claimed medium-severity Subscriber information-disclosure does not hold. The specific saving check is the AjaxController::validateToken nonce enforcement combined with the nonce being scoped to capability-gated admin contexts and bound to the issuing user's session.

On a default single-site install the only actor with access is an administrator, who already has unfiltered_html and can natively inject raw

all.title: Predictable session identifier derived from static salt and user id
all.vuln_class: Predictable session token / weak session management
all.data_flow: On construction the session hash is computed as 'n2' . md5(self::$salt . $userIdentifier) where self::$salt is the hardcoded constant 'nextendSalt' (line 12) and $userIdentifier is get_current_user_id() (WordPressStorage.php:14). For unauthenticated visitors user id is 0, so the hash is a fixed, publicly computable value (md5('nextendSalt0')) shared by all anonymous visitors; for any logged-in user the value is derivable from the (often enumerable) numeric user id. The session is persisted via WordPress transients (WordPressStorage.php) and the cookie domain is taken from the attacker-influenceable HTTP_HOST header (line 26). Impact is limited because the session storage in this plugin only holds transient admin notification messages rather than authentication state or sensitive data, but the identifier provides no real entropy or per-session secret.

verdict.auth_required: none (unauthenticated)
verdict.reasoning:
The code description in the claim is factually accurate. In /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/.claude/worktrees/woo-security-review/plugins/src/smart-slider-3/Nextend/Framework/Session/AbstractStorage.php line 25 the session hash is computed as 'n2' . md5(self::$salt . $userIdentifier) where $salt is the hardcoded constant 'nextendSalt' (line 12) and $userIdentifier is get_current_user_id() (WordPressStorage.php:14). For anonymous visitors the value is fixed (md5('nextendSalt0')) and for logged-in users it is derivable from the numeric user id. So the identifier genuinely lacks entropy.

However, this is not a realistically exploitable vulnerability:

1. NO SENSITIVE DATA OR AUTH STATE: The session storage holds only transient admin UI notification text. Grepping all Session::set/Session::get usage shows the ONLY consumer is Nextend/Framework/Notification/Notification.php, which stores three arrays: 'error', 'success', 'notice'. These are flash messages rendered in the admin notices area. There is no authentication token, no capability grant, no PII, no secret. Knowing the hash gives an attacker nothing of value.

2. NO ATTACKER-USABLE READ/WRITE PRIMITIVE: The session is persisted via WordPress transients (get_transient/set_transient, WordPressStorage.php). An unauthenticated web attacker cannot read or write an arbitrary transient by key — there is no exposed endpoint that returns or sets transient contents by attacker-chosen name. Writes only ever occur for the requesting context's own computed hash (Session::set within the current request), so the predictable id cannot be used to inject content into another user's session bucket over the web. Reading another user's transient requires direct DB/object-cache access, which is not part of any web-facing flow.

3. SELF-CONCEDED LOW IMPACT: The claim itself rates severity 'low' and states 'Impact is limited because the session storage in this plugin only holds transient admin notification messages rather than authentication state or sensitive data.' No exploit path produces a security-relevant outcome (no session fixation of auth, no privilege escalation, no data disclosure).

4. The setcookie HTTP_HOST argument (line 26) and shared anonymous bucket are minor design quirks but, given the non-sensitive payload and absence of an arbitrary read/write primitive, yield no real attacker advantage.

This is a legitimate weak-session-design / weak-crypto code-quality observation, but it is not an exploitable vulnerability on a default/common install. is_real = false; severity = none.

all.title: Missing capability check in ControllerPreview::actionFull (inconsistent with sibling preview actions)
all.vuln_class: Broken Access Control / Missing Capability Check
all.auth_required: Authenticated user holding the admin-only 'nextend_security' nonce (effectively a user with 'smartslider' view access but possibly not 'smartslider_edit')
all.data_flow: wp_ajax_smart-slider3 (AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax -> processRequest) routes nextendcontroller=preview&nextendaction=full to ControllerPreview::actionFull(). actionFull() calls only validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce of 'nextend_security'); unlike actionIndex/actionSlider/actionSlide/actionGenerator it omits validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). Attacker-controlled POST sliderData/slidesData/generatorData are json_decode'd and passed to ViewPreviewFull -> SliderManager->render() in admin mode (renderSlider, ViewPreviewFull.php:98-105), which renders the supplied configuration without any further capability gate. The other four preview actions enforce smartslider_edit, so this is a privilege-boundary inconsistency: a principal with the nonce but without smartslider_edit can render arbitrary slider/generator preview output.

verdict.auth_required: In practice administrator. The nextend_security nonce is only emitted on Smart Slider admin pages gated by the 'smartslider' capability + unfiltered_html, and those caps (smartslider/smartslider_edit/smartslider_delete) are added ONLY to the administrator role at install with no map_meta_cap to lesser roles.
verdict.reasoning: REFUTED. The claimed principal (holds the nextend_security nonce but lacks smartslider_edit) does not exist on a default/common install. InstallWordPress.php:13-16 grants smartslider, smartslider_config, smartslider_edit, smartslider_delete ONLY to the administrator role; there is no map_meta_cap/user_has_cap mapping these to lower roles. The nonce is created only via PlatformForm::tokenize() (wp_create_nonce('nextend_security')) which is rendered only on Smart Slider admin pages — and those pages require the 'smartslider' menu capability plus checkForCap()/unfiltered_html. WP nonces are session-bound: a low-privilege user who never receives a Smart Slider admin page cannot generate or forge a valid 'nextend_security' nonce. Therefore any user who can satisfy validateToken() on actionFull() is an administrator who ALREADY holds smartslider_edit, so the missing validatePermission('smartslider_edit') is never the sole effective barrier for any realistic user. The privilege boundary the claim relies on (nonce without smartslider_edit) is only constructible via a hand-crafted, non-default custom role that grants 'smartslider' menu access but withholds smartslider_edit — not a default/common install. Even in that contrived case the impact is negligible: ControllerPreview::actionFull -> ViewPreviewFull::renderSlider -> SliderManager::render() renders the attacker's own POSTed sliderData/slidesData/generatorData transiently and returns the HTML to the SAME caller (no DB write, no persistence, no cross-user delivery — verified SliderManager::render has no $wpdb/insert/update). That is self-rendering of one's own input (self-XSS at worst), not a cross-privilege escalation. The data-persisting actions are in separate controllers that do enforce smartslider_edit (AdminVisualManagerAjaxController). The code inconsistency is genuine as hygiene (siblings actionIndex/actionSlider/actionSlide/actionGenerator check smartslider_edit, actionFull does not), but it is not an exploitable broken-access-control vulnerability for any real attacker on a default/common install. The save that does NOT happen here is what matters: no sensitive read of other users' data and no write occur.

all.title: Path traversal / arbitrary file delete via unvalidated local-import-file in slider import
all.auth_required: admin (smartslider_edit + unfiltered_html capabilities, granted only to administrators by InstallWordPress.php) and valid CSRF token
all.data_flow: actionImport(): $data = new Data(Request::$REQUEST->getVar('slider')); $_file = $data->get('local-import-file'); $file = Platform::getPublicDirectory() . '/' . $_file (line 285). The value is taken verbatim from POST with no basename(), realpath() containment, or '..' filtering. SelectFile only populates the dropdown via scandir() client-side; the server never validates that the submitted value is one of the listed files. $file then flows to Filesystem::fileexists($file) (line 289) and ImportSlider::import($file, ...) which reads it via Reader::read(); on a successful import, if $data->get('delete') is set, @unlink($file) (line 304) deletes the attacker-chosen path. A value like '../../../../some/dir/file.ss3' escapes the intended public directory. With the delete flag, any reachable file (that parses as a valid .ss3 archive) can be unlinked, and arbitrary paths can be probed/read during import.

verdict.auth_required: Administrator (requires both smartslider_edit and unfiltered_html capabilities, granted only to the administrator role) plus a valid CSRF nonce.
verdict.reasoning:
The claimed data flow is technically accurate. In ControllerAjaxSliders::actionImport, $_file = $data->get('local-import-file') returns the raw POST value with NO sanitization (Data::get at Data.php:67 returns the value verbatim; the typed getCmd accessor that would strip non-word chars is NOT used). It is concatenated at line 285 as Platform::getPublicDirectory() . '/' . $_file with no basename(), no realpath() containment, and no '..' filtering. It then flows to Filesystem::fileexists() which is simply is_file() (line 156) -- note safefileexists() with realpath() exists but is deliberately NOT used here -- and on a successful .ss3 import with the delete flag set, @unlink($file) runs at line 304. A value like ../../../../path/file.ss3 does escape the uploads directory. So the missing-sanitizer code defect is REAL.

However, the full exploit path does NOT cross any privilege boundary, so it is not a meaningful vulnerability. actionImport (line 247-251) enforces validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce on nextend_nonce / nextend_security -- a real CSRF check) AND validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). canDo resolves to WordPressAcl::canDo = current_user_can($action) && current_user_can('unfiltered_html') (WordPressAcl.php:11). smartslider_edit is granted ONLY to the administrator role (InstallWordPress.php:15), and unfiltered_html is administrator-only on default single-site installs. Thus only an authenticated administrator with a valid nonce can reach the sink.

An administrator already possesses full filesystem control in WordPress (plugin/theme file editor, plugin/theme upload and deletion, etc.) and can delete or read arbitrary server-readable files through entirely legitimate built-in means. The path traversal grants no capability beyond what the required privilege already confers, and CSRF is blocked by the nonce. There is no lower-privilege actor (subscriber/author/editor) who can trigger this. Per the standard that is_real requires a real attacker at the stated privilege to gain something exploitable on a default install, this fails: it is an admin-only, CSRF-protected, post-auth operation by a user who is already trusted with the filesystem. The genuine defect is the absence of basename()/realpath() containment, which is a defense-in-depth/code-quality issue (low severity), not an exploitable security boundary crossing.

all.title: PHP Object Injection via raw unserialize() on post meta in extractPostMeta()
all.auth_required: low-privileged (author/contributor who can set post meta) for the source; admin renders/regenerates the slider
all.data_flow: get_post_meta($post->ID) [PostsPosts::_getData L340 / PostsPostsByIDs::_getData L87 / PostsCustomPosts::_getData L453] -> GeneratorGroupPosts::extractPostMeta($post_meta) -> foreach value $v -> is_serialized($v) (L91) -> unserialize($v) (L92). The meta value $v originates from a WordPress post that is included by a Posts/Posts-by-IDs/Custom-Posts generator. A lower-privileged user (or any plugin) that can store a crafted serialized string in a non-protected post meta key controls $v. When the slider is rendered or the generator preview is fetched, raw unserialize() instantiates arbitrary PHP objects, enabling a POP gadget chain (RCE/file write/SQLi) if a suitable gadget exists in WP core or another active plugin. The code uses unserialize() directly rather than maybe_unserialize() with allowed_classes=>false.

verdict.auth_required: Would require a low-privileged user (author/contributor able to set custom post meta) to store a payload AND an admin to configure/render a Posts generator referencing that post; not exploitable on a default install.
verdict.reasoning:
The sink is genuine: GeneratorGroupPosts::extractPostMeta() at line 92 calls raw unserialize($v) with no allowed_classes=>false, instead of maybe_unserialize. Source is get_post_meta($post->ID) called from PostsPosts/PostsPostsByIDs/PostsCustomPosts _getData(). However the full POI exploit path does NOT hold on a default/common install:

1) WP core defense missed by reviewer: get_post_meta($id) (no key) returns every value already passed through maybe_unserialize() by core on retrieval. A normally-stored serialized OBJECT therefore arrives as an actual object and is rejected at line 79 (!is_object($v)); a serialized ARRAY arrives as an array and is rejected (!is_array($v)). So a single-layer serialized-object payload can never reach line 92 as a string. The only way to reach the sink with a serialized-object STRING is a double-serialized DB value (literal s:N:"O:8:...";) so core's single maybe_unserialize peels one layer and yields the inner object-string. This is contrived and requires storing an exact literal double-serialized string in a non-excluded, non-protected meta key.

2) Even if an object IS instantiated, the code only consumes is_array() results and extracts string scalars (lines 94-108); the object is discarded. Impact depends entirely on __wakeup/__destruct POP gadgets fired during unserialize().

3) No usable POP gadget chain exists in default WordPress core (historical ones were patched). RCE/file-write/SQLi would require a coincidental vulnerable third-party plugin, not present on a default install.

4) Trigger requires admin action (configuring and rendering/previewing a Posts generator against the attacker's post), and the attacker must be able to set arbitrary meta keys/values.

This is a real defense-in-depth hardening gap (should use unserialize($v, ['allowed_classes'=>false]) or maybe_unserialize with allowed_classes), but it is not a practically exploitable PHP Object Injection on a default/common install. Severity is overstated as medium; treat as low. Saving factors: WP core maybe_unserialize on retrieval + line 79 is_object/is_array guards + absence of a default-install gadget chain.

all.title: Stored XSS via single-quote breakout in SlideEvent onclick handler
all.auth_required: admin to configure the slideevent[...] link; effective payload can be supplied by a lower-privileged author/contributor through generator variables (post content/meta) substituted into the link
all.data_flow: A layer link of the form 'slideevent[<argument>]' is parsed by Framework/Parser/Link::parse() (L28-33) which extracts <argument> (matches[2]) and passes it to SlideEvent::parse($argument). Before parsing, the link is run through Slide::fill() (AbstractItemFrontend::getLink L102-103, Slide::fill L513) which substitutes generator variables such as {title}/{content}/{meta...} drawn from WordPress posts (attacker-influenced content). SlideEvent::parse concatenates $argument directly into $attributes['onclick'] = "n2ss.trigger(this, '" . $argument . "', event);" with no escaping. The onclick attribute is later emitted by Html::renderAttributes() (Framework/View/Html.php L142) using encodeAttribute() which calls htmlspecialchars with ENT_COMPAT (L162-167) and deliberately does NOT encode single quotes. An attacker-controlled $argument containing a single quote, e.g. "');alert(document.cookie);//", breaks out of the JS string literal inside the rendered onclick="..." and executes arbitrary JavaScript on the front end for every visitor.

verdict.auth_required: admin (to configure the slideevent[...] link). The lower-privileged author/contributor path requires a non-default admin precondition that does not hold on a common install.
verdict.reasoning:
The raw technical chain is real and was verified: SlideEvent::parse (line 14) concatenates $argument into onclick="n2ss.trigger(this, '" . $argument . "', event);" with no escaping; Html::encodeAttribute (Html.php L162-167) uses htmlspecialchars with ENT_COMPAT | ENT_HTML5, which deliberately does NOT encode single quotes, while the attribute is emitted wrapped in double quotes (L142). A single quote in $argument therefore breaks out of the JS string literal. Slide::fill -> parseVariable (Slide.php L565) returns generator variable values verbatim with no escaping, so generator data is substituted raw. So the sink and encoder gap are genuine.

What defeats is_real on a default/common install is the attacker model, not a sanitizer. (1) Reaching this sink requires an admin to have explicitly configured a layer href as the slideevent[...] link type. The Link::parse javascript: block (L25) does not save it because this is the slideevent[...] branch, not a URL. (2) The admin-only case (admin types slideevent['); alert(1)//] directly) is admin self-XSS: Smart Slider 3 admins already configure raw JavaScript through numerous callback/JS fields, so an admin injecting JS into their own slider is not a privilege boundary crossing. (3) The lower-privileged (author/contributor) escalation the claim relies on requires the admin to have placed an attacker-influenced generator variable (e.g. {title}/{content}) INSIDE a slideevent[] argument (slideevent[{title}]). The normal use of slideevent[] is a static, admin-chosen event name; putting generator post data into the JS-event argument is a contrived, non-default configuration with no evidence of being common. Absent that specific admin setup, attacker-controlled post content never reaches this onclick sink.

Net: a genuine missing-escape code smell (ENT_COMPAT in a JS-context attribute plus unescaped concatenation), but no realistic exploit path for a non-admin attacker on a default/common install; the default-config impact reduces to admin self-XSS. Hence is_real=false, severity low.

all.title: Stored XSS via unescaped Vimeo 'start' value concatenated into inline JavaScript
all.auth_required: User able to edit slider/slide layer data (typically admin/editor), or attacker controlling dynamic generator variables substituted by Slide::fill()
all.data_flow: ItemVimeo layer field 'start' (stored slide item value) -> ItemVimeoFrontend::render() reads $this->data->get('start','0') -> passed through $owner->fill() (Slide::fill(), which performs {variable}/{func()} template substitution and does NOT HTML/JS-escape, and can inject generator-sourced/external values) -> concatenated RAW (no json_encode, no quotes, no intval) as the final argument of the addScript() string: new _N2.FrontendItemVimeo(this, "id", "elementID", <toJSON>, <hasImage>, <start>); -> addScript() pushes the string into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks -> AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118/125) does implode($initCallbacks) and embeds it verbatim inside a JS function body: "initCallbacks":function(){ ...<start>... } -> emitted into the frontend page and executed by the browser. A 'start' value such as `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the function-call argument and runs arbitrary JS. Note the sibling YouTube item (ItemYouTubeFrontend.php line 119) handles the identical 'start' value safely by routing it only through json_encode via toJSON(), confirming this raw concatenation is a defect; the Number form-field constraint is client-side only and not enforced server-side at this sink.

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated user with the plugin's `smartslider_edit` capability (admin/editor) plus a valid CSRF token; the injected payload then executes for unauthenticated frontend visitors (stored XSS, not pure self-XSS).
verdict.reasoning:
The full exploit path holds end to end.

SINK (confirmed): ItemVimeoFrontend.php line 102 builds the init script by RAW concatenation of the last argument: `... , ' . $hasImage . ', ' . $owner->fill($this->data->get('start', '0')) . ');'` — no quotes, no json_encode, no intval. This string is pushed via addScript() into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks (AbstractRenderable.php:137), then AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118 implode, line 125) embeds it VERBATIM inside `"initCallbacks":function(){ ... }` which is emitted to the page and executed. A value like `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the call and runs arbitrary JS.

SOURCE / no sanitizer on any path:
- Save path: ControllerAjaxSlides::actionEdit() -> ModelSlides::save() -> convertSlideDataToDatabaseRow() (ModelSlides.php:658) stores `$row['slide'] = $slideData['layers']` (the full layer tree incl. `start`) as json_encode'd JSON with NO per-field sanitization. The `Number` form field (`min => 0`) in ItemVimeo::renderFields() is a client-side-only constraint, never enforced server-side at this sink.
- Slide::fill() (Slide.php:513) returns the value unchanged when no variables, or does {variable}/{func()} template substitution — neither path HTML/JS-escapes, so it cannot save the sink.
- The sink itself applies no escaping.

SIBLING PROOF: ItemYouTubeFrontend.php handles the identical `start` value safely by routing it only through toJSON()/json_encode (line 119, never raw-concatenated), confirming the Vimeo raw concatenation is a real defect, not intended design.

AUTH: actionEdit requires validateToken() (CSRF nonce) and validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). That gates exploitation to a privileged editing user, but the payload is stored and runs on the PUBLIC frontend for all visitors and any other admin viewing the slider — so it is genuine stored XSS reaching lower-privilege victims, not self-XSS. WP core defenses do not help: the value is placed in a JS execution context (not HTML), so wp_kses/esc_html are irrelevant, and the plugin applies none anyway.

Severity medium is correct: real and exploitable, but requires an authenticated slider-editing capability to plant the payload.

all_with_verdicts.title: Media directory listing AJAX action lacks capability check (only nonce required)
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Authenticated (any logged-in user who can obtain the 'nextend_security' nonce)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: wp_ajax_smart-slider3 -> AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax() -> display_controller('sliders','index',true) -> AbstractApplicationType::processRequest() reads attacker-controlled nextendcontroller/nextendaction from request -> dispatches ControllerAjaxBrowse::actionIndex(). actionIndex() only calls validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce with the static action 'nextend_security') and performs NO current_user_can() check (unlike actionUpload which checks 'upload_files'). The page-render path enforces checkForCap()/unfiltered_html, but the AJAX path (display_admin_ajax) does not. The handler then takes $_REQUEST['path'], builds a directory under the uploads/images folder, and returns directory + file (jpg/png/gif/svg/mp4/...) listings with their URLs. realpath()+strpos($path,$root)===0 mitigate path traversal for non-symlink paths, so the exposure is limited to enumerating the contents/URLs of the media/images folder rather than arbitrary filesystem read.

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated, low-privilege (Contributor/Author with edit_posts or edit_pages, which lets them obtain the nextend_security nonce via the TinyMCE editor integration). No upload_files or admin/unfiltered_html capability needed.
verdict.reasoning:
Verified the full chain. wp_ajax_smart-slider3 -> AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax() (AdminHelper.php:155-158) does NOT call checkForCap(), unlike every page-render handler. It calls display_controller('sliders','index',true) -> AbstractApplicationType::processRequest() which reads attacker-controlled nextendcontroller/nextendaction from Request::getCmd() (AbstractApplicationType.php:66-76). getControllerAjaxBrowse() returns ControllerAjaxBrowse (ApplicationTypeAdmin.php:161-163). actionIndex() (line 17) calls ONLY validateToken() = wp_verify_nonce(nextend_nonce,'nextend_security') (PlatformForm.php:21-22); no current_user_can() — confirmed by contrast with actionUpload() at line 145 which requires upload_files. It then takes $_REQUEST['path'] and returns directory + media-file listings with URLs.

Nonce obtainability for non-admins: HelperTinyMCE::addButton() (admin_init hook) emits wp_create_nonce('nextend_security') via Predefined::backend() for any user with edit_posts OR edit_pages on the post/page editor screens (HelperTinyMCE.php:28-70). Since the nonce action is a static string (not capability-bound) and wp_verify_nonce only validates action+session, a Contributor/Author can generate a valid nonce for their own session and call the Browse action — a genuine privilege gap (they typically lack upload_files).

Defenses that DO apply: realpath()+strpos($path,$root)!==0 (lines 24-26) constrain enumeration to the uploads images folder for non-symlink paths, and the nonce keeps it authenticated. These limit impact to media-directory enumeration (names + URLs), not arbitrary file read — consistent with low severity. No capability-check sanitizer saves the AJAX path; the missing current_user_can() is real. Claim confirmed.

all_with_verdicts.title: WP_Query query-var injection via unsanitized search keyword (auth info disclosure of non-public posts)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: WordPress query-var injection / broken access control (information disclosure)
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: low-privileged authenticated user (e.g. Subscriber). The handler is registered on add_action('wp_ajax_smart-slider3', ...) in Nextend/SmartSlider3/Platform/WordPress/Admin/AdminHelper.php:35 and dispatched via processRequest() which reads the controller/action from request params; display_admin_ajax() (AdminHelper.php:155) does NOT call checkForCap(), and actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent only call validateToken() (a shared 'nextend_security' nonce) with no validatePermission(). So any logged-in user who can obtain the nonce reaches it.
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Request::$REQUEST->getVar('keyword') (raw, only stripslashed - Nextend/Framework/Request/Parser/WordPressRequestParser.php:19, Storage.php:50) -> ControllerAjaxContent::actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent (ControllerAjaxContent.php:15,22) -> Content::searchLink/searchContent -> WordPressContent::searchLink line 20 / searchContent line 45: new WP_Query('post_type=any&posts_per_page=20&post_status=publish&s=' . $keyword). The string-form WP_Query argument is parsed with wp_parse_args/parse_str, so a keyword containing '&' and '=' injects/overrides arbitrary query vars. e.g. keyword='x&post_status=private,draft,pending' overrides the earlier post_status=publish, causing the query to return non-public posts; their title, excerpt, permalink and attachment image are returned in the AJAX response. Other query vars (e.g. p, page_id, post__in) can likewise be injected to enumerate specific posts.

verdict.auth_required: Effectively requires Smart Slider admin/editor capability (unfiltered_html / edit_posts / smartslider). A Subscriber CANNOT reach the sink because they cannot obtain a valid nextend_security nonce. The claimed Subscriber-level exploit is not achievable.
verdict.reasoning:
The query-var injection mechanism itself is real: WordPressContent::searchLink/searchContent (lines 20/45) use the STRING form new WP_Query('post_type=any&posts_per_page=20&post_status=publish&s=' . $keyword), which WP_Query parses via wp_parse_args/parse_str, so a keyword containing &post_status=private overrides the earlier post_status. The source (Request::getVar('keyword')) is only stripslashed, never sanitized (WordPressRequestParser.php:19). Routing is reachable: processRequest reads nextendcontroller/nextendaction from the request (AbstractApplicationType.php:66-71), so the hardcoded display_admin_ajax -> ('sliders','index') can be redirected to Content/SearchLink, and getControllerAjaxContent() exists (ApplicationTypeAdmin.php:151). The claim is also correct that display_admin_ajax() does NOT call checkForCap() and the actions call only validateToken(), not validatePermission().

What REFUTES the exploit at the stated (Subscriber) privilege level is the nonce gate. ControllerAjaxContent::actionSearchLink/actionSearchContent call $this->validateToken(), which resolves to the AjaxController override (AjaxController.php:29-35) that calls $this->response->error() on nonce failure and TERMINATES (the base AbstractController version merely returns false, but that is not the one used here). validateToken -> Form::checkToken -> wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['nextend_nonce'], 'nextend_security') (PlatformForm.php:22). WordPress nonces are bound to the current user ID + session, so a nonce minted for an admin is invalid under a Subscriber's session.

The nextend_security nonce is emitted ONLY by Predefined::backend() (Predefined.php:31, via Form::tokenizeUrl()) and admin form rendering, all of which run exclusively in admin/editor contexts that require smartslider / edit_posts / unfiltered_html capability (SmartSlider3PlatformWordPress.php:48-62 gates on is_admin()/Gutenberg editor; AbstractAdminController and admin_head paths). Critically, Predefined::frontend() — the version invoked by the front-end controller initialize() (AbstractController.php:125) and public slider rendering — does NOT call tokenizeUrl() and therefore does NOT expose the nonce. There is no front-end or low-privilege code path that mints nextend_security for a Subscriber's own session. Consequently a Subscriber cannot obtain a usable nonce and cannot reach the sink.

Only users who already hold Smart Slider admin/editor capability (unfiltered_html/edit_posts) can pass validateToken. Those users can already read all posts (including private/draft) through normal WP admin, so the post_status override discloses nothing beyond their existing authorization — no privilege boundary is crossed. The claimed medium-severity Subscriber information-disclosure does not hold. The specific saving check is the AjaxController::validateToken nonce enforcement combined with the nonce being scoped to capability-gated admin contexts and bound to the issuing user's session.

all_with_verdicts.title: Case-sensitive javascript: check and incomplete attribute sanitization in custom HTML filter (filter_attributes_on)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Slide item content (heading/text/button) authored via admin editor is stored, then on the frontend passed through Sanitize::filter_allowed_html() -> filter_attributes_on() in ItemHeadingFrontend.php:48, ItemTextFrontend.php:42/60/86, ItemButtonFrontend.php:34. filter_attributes_on() only removes attributes whose name starts with 'on' and only strips href values that contain the exact lowercase substring 'javascript:' (strpos at line 684 is case-sensitive). A value like href="JaVaScRiPt:alert(document.cookie)" or use of other vectors (e.g. with data:/vbscript:, or other URL-bearing allowed attributes such as / which are never checked) survives the filter and is rendered verbatim on the public frontend, executing in the context of every site visitor.

verdict.auth_required: admin/editor (smartslider_edit capability; by default only administrators, and admin UI also gates on unfiltered_html)
verdict.reasoning: Code confirmed at /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/.claude/worktrees/woo-security-review/plugins/src/smart-slider-3/Nextend/Framework/Sanitize.php:684. The href check uses strpos (case-sensitive), not stripos, so href="JaVaScRiPt:alert(...)" survives filter_attributes_on() and is rendered verbatim on the frontend via Html::tag in ItemHeadingFrontend.php:48, ItemTextFrontend.php:42/60/86, ItemButtonFrontend.php:34. filter_allowed_html() is the ONLY sanitizer on that path (no later wp_kses). The case-sensitive javascript: bypass is therefore a REAL sanitizer-bypass. Caveats lowering impact: (1) The on* attribute removal at line 677 correctly strips onerror/onload etc. for every tag, so the strongest auto-firing vector is closed. (2) The claim's / vector is INCORRECT — javascript: in img/source src does not execute in any modern browser; vbscript: is dead and top-level data: navigation is blocked. So the only genuine survivor is , which requires a victim CLICK (not stored auto-XSS). Threat model / privilege boundary: smartslider_edit is granted by default ONLY to administrator (InstallWordPress.php:10-16) and the admin UI gates on current_user_can('unfiltered_html') (AdminHelper.php:116). On a default single-site install the only actor with access is an administrator, who already has unfiltered_html and can natively inject raw

all_with_verdicts.title: Predictable session identifier derived from static salt and user id
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Predictable session token / weak session management
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: On construction the session hash is computed as 'n2' . md5(self::$salt . $userIdentifier) where self::$salt is the hardcoded constant 'nextendSalt' (line 12) and $userIdentifier is get_current_user_id() (WordPressStorage.php:14). For unauthenticated visitors user id is 0, so the hash is a fixed, publicly computable value (md5('nextendSalt0')) shared by all anonymous visitors; for any logged-in user the value is derivable from the (often enumerable) numeric user id. The session is persisted via WordPress transients (WordPressStorage.php) and the cookie domain is taken from the attacker-influenceable HTTP_HOST header (line 26). Impact is limited because the session storage in this plugin only holds transient admin notification messages rather than authentication state or sensitive data, but the identifier provides no real entropy or per-session secret.

verdict.auth_required: none (unauthenticated)
verdict.reasoning:
The code description in the claim is factually accurate. In /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/.claude/worktrees/woo-security-review/plugins/src/smart-slider-3/Nextend/Framework/Session/AbstractStorage.php line 25 the session hash is computed as 'n2' . md5(self::$salt . $userIdentifier) where $salt is the hardcoded constant 'nextendSalt' (line 12) and $userIdentifier is get_current_user_id() (WordPressStorage.php:14). For anonymous visitors the value is fixed (md5('nextendSalt0')) and for logged-in users it is derivable from the numeric user id. So the identifier genuinely lacks entropy.

However, this is not a realistically exploitable vulnerability:

1. NO SENSITIVE DATA OR AUTH STATE: The session storage holds only transient admin UI notification text. Grepping all Session::set/Session::get usage shows the ONLY consumer is Nextend/Framework/Notification/Notification.php, which stores three arrays: 'error', 'success', 'notice'. These are flash messages rendered in the admin notices area. There is no authentication token, no capability grant, no PII, no secret. Knowing the hash gives an attacker nothing of value.

2. NO ATTACKER-USABLE READ/WRITE PRIMITIVE: The session is persisted via WordPress transients (get_transient/set_transient, WordPressStorage.php). An unauthenticated web attacker cannot read or write an arbitrary transient by key — there is no exposed endpoint that returns or sets transient contents by attacker-chosen name. Writes only ever occur for the requesting context's own computed hash (Session::set within the current request), so the predictable id cannot be used to inject content into another user's session bucket over the web. Reading another user's transient requires direct DB/object-cache access, which is not part of any web-facing flow.

3. SELF-CONCEDED LOW IMPACT: The claim itself rates severity 'low' and states 'Impact is limited because the session storage in this plugin only holds transient admin notification messages rather than authentication state or sensitive data.' No exploit path produces a security-relevant outcome (no session fixation of auth, no privilege escalation, no data disclosure).

4. The setcookie HTTP_HOST argument (line 26) and shared anonymous bucket are minor design quirks but, given the non-sensitive payload and absence of an arbitrary read/write primitive, yield no real attacker advantage.

This is a legitimate weak-session-design / weak-crypto code-quality observation, but it is not an exploitable vulnerability on a default/common install. is_real = false; severity = none.

all_with_verdicts.title: Missing capability check in ControllerPreview::actionFull (inconsistent with sibling preview actions)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Broken Access Control / Missing Capability Check
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Authenticated user holding the admin-only 'nextend_security' nonce (effectively a user with 'smartslider' view access but possibly not 'smartslider_edit')
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: wp_ajax_smart-slider3 (AdminHelper::display_admin_ajax -> processRequest) routes nextendcontroller=preview&nextendaction=full to ControllerPreview::actionFull(). actionFull() calls only validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce of 'nextend_security'); unlike actionIndex/actionSlider/actionSlide/actionGenerator it omits validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). Attacker-controlled POST sliderData/slidesData/generatorData are json_decode'd and passed to ViewPreviewFull -> SliderManager->render() in admin mode (renderSlider, ViewPreviewFull.php:98-105), which renders the supplied configuration without any further capability gate. The other four preview actions enforce smartslider_edit, so this is a privilege-boundary inconsistency: a principal with the nonce but without smartslider_edit can render arbitrary slider/generator preview output.

verdict.auth_required: In practice administrator. The nextend_security nonce is only emitted on Smart Slider admin pages gated by the 'smartslider' capability + unfiltered_html, and those caps (smartslider/smartslider_edit/smartslider_delete) are added ONLY to the administrator role at install with no map_meta_cap to lesser roles.
verdict.reasoning: REFUTED. The claimed principal (holds the nextend_security nonce but lacks smartslider_edit) does not exist on a default/common install. InstallWordPress.php:13-16 grants smartslider, smartslider_config, smartslider_edit, smartslider_delete ONLY to the administrator role; there is no map_meta_cap/user_has_cap mapping these to lower roles. The nonce is created only via PlatformForm::tokenize() (wp_create_nonce('nextend_security')) which is rendered only on Smart Slider admin pages — and those pages require the 'smartslider' menu capability plus checkForCap()/unfiltered_html. WP nonces are session-bound: a low-privilege user who never receives a Smart Slider admin page cannot generate or forge a valid 'nextend_security' nonce. Therefore any user who can satisfy validateToken() on actionFull() is an administrator who ALREADY holds smartslider_edit, so the missing validatePermission('smartslider_edit') is never the sole effective barrier for any realistic user. The privilege boundary the claim relies on (nonce without smartslider_edit) is only constructible via a hand-crafted, non-default custom role that grants 'smartslider' menu access but withholds smartslider_edit — not a default/common install. Even in that contrived case the impact is negligible: ControllerPreview::actionFull -> ViewPreviewFull::renderSlider -> SliderManager::render() renders the attacker's own POSTed sliderData/slidesData/generatorData transiently and returns the HTML to the SAME caller (no DB write, no persistence, no cross-user delivery — verified SliderManager::render has no $wpdb/insert/update). That is self-rendering of one's own input (self-XSS at worst), not a cross-privilege escalation. The data-persisting actions are in separate controllers that do enforce smartslider_edit (AdminVisualManagerAjaxController). The code inconsistency is genuine as hygiene (siblings actionIndex/actionSlider/actionSlide/actionGenerator check smartslider_edit, actionFull does not), but it is not an exploitable broken-access-control vulnerability for any real attacker on a default/common install. The save that does NOT happen here is what matters: no sensitive read of other users' data and no write occur.

all_with_verdicts.title: Path traversal / arbitrary file delete via unvalidated local-import-file in slider import
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: admin (smartslider_edit + unfiltered_html capabilities, granted only to administrators by InstallWordPress.php) and valid CSRF token
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: actionImport(): $data = new Data(Request::$REQUEST->getVar('slider')); $_file = $data->get('local-import-file'); $file = Platform::getPublicDirectory() . '/' . $_file (line 285). The value is taken verbatim from POST with no basename(), realpath() containment, or '..' filtering. SelectFile only populates the dropdown via scandir() client-side; the server never validates that the submitted value is one of the listed files. $file then flows to Filesystem::fileexists($file) (line 289) and ImportSlider::import($file, ...) which reads it via Reader::read(); on a successful import, if $data->get('delete') is set, @unlink($file) (line 304) deletes the attacker-chosen path. A value like '../../../../some/dir/file.ss3' escapes the intended public directory. With the delete flag, any reachable file (that parses as a valid .ss3 archive) can be unlinked, and arbitrary paths can be probed/read during import.

verdict.auth_required: Administrator (requires both smartslider_edit and unfiltered_html capabilities, granted only to the administrator role) plus a valid CSRF nonce.
verdict.reasoning:
The claimed data flow is technically accurate. In ControllerAjaxSliders::actionImport, $_file = $data->get('local-import-file') returns the raw POST value with NO sanitization (Data::get at Data.php:67 returns the value verbatim; the typed getCmd accessor that would strip non-word chars is NOT used). It is concatenated at line 285 as Platform::getPublicDirectory() . '/' . $_file with no basename(), no realpath() containment, and no '..' filtering. It then flows to Filesystem::fileexists() which is simply is_file() (line 156) -- note safefileexists() with realpath() exists but is deliberately NOT used here -- and on a successful .ss3 import with the delete flag set, @unlink($file) runs at line 304. A value like ../../../../path/file.ss3 does escape the uploads directory. So the missing-sanitizer code defect is REAL.

However, the full exploit path does NOT cross any privilege boundary, so it is not a meaningful vulnerability. actionImport (line 247-251) enforces validateToken() (wp_verify_nonce on nextend_nonce / nextend_security -- a real CSRF check) AND validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). canDo resolves to WordPressAcl::canDo = current_user_can($action) && current_user_can('unfiltered_html') (WordPressAcl.php:11). smartslider_edit is granted ONLY to the administrator role (InstallWordPress.php:15), and unfiltered_html is administrator-only on default single-site installs. Thus only an authenticated administrator with a valid nonce can reach the sink.

An administrator already possesses full filesystem control in WordPress (plugin/theme file editor, plugin/theme upload and deletion, etc.) and can delete or read arbitrary server-readable files through entirely legitimate built-in means. The path traversal grants no capability beyond what the required privilege already confers, and CSRF is blocked by the nonce. There is no lower-privilege actor (subscriber/author/editor) who can trigger this. Per the standard that is_real requires a real attacker at the stated privilege to gain something exploitable on a default install, this fails: it is an admin-only, CSRF-protected, post-auth operation by a user who is already trusted with the filesystem. The genuine defect is the absence of basename()/realpath() containment, which is a defense-in-depth/code-quality issue (low severity), not an exploitable security boundary crossing.

all_with_verdicts.title: PHP Object Injection via raw unserialize() on post meta in extractPostMeta()
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: low-privileged (author/contributor who can set post meta) for the source; admin renders/regenerates the slider
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: get_post_meta($post->ID) [PostsPosts::_getData L340 / PostsPostsByIDs::_getData L87 / PostsCustomPosts::_getData L453] -> GeneratorGroupPosts::extractPostMeta($post_meta) -> foreach value $v -> is_serialized($v) (L91) -> unserialize($v) (L92). The meta value $v originates from a WordPress post that is included by a Posts/Posts-by-IDs/Custom-Posts generator. A lower-privileged user (or any plugin) that can store a crafted serialized string in a non-protected post meta key controls $v. When the slider is rendered or the generator preview is fetched, raw unserialize() instantiates arbitrary PHP objects, enabling a POP gadget chain (RCE/file write/SQLi) if a suitable gadget exists in WP core or another active plugin. The code uses unserialize() directly rather than maybe_unserialize() with allowed_classes=>false.

verdict.auth_required: Would require a low-privileged user (author/contributor able to set custom post meta) to store a payload AND an admin to configure/render a Posts generator referencing that post; not exploitable on a default install.
verdict.reasoning:
The sink is genuine: GeneratorGroupPosts::extractPostMeta() at line 92 calls raw unserialize($v) with no allowed_classes=>false, instead of maybe_unserialize. Source is get_post_meta($post->ID) called from PostsPosts/PostsPostsByIDs/PostsCustomPosts _getData(). However the full POI exploit path does NOT hold on a default/common install:

1) WP core defense missed by reviewer: get_post_meta($id) (no key) returns every value already passed through maybe_unserialize() by core on retrieval. A normally-stored serialized OBJECT therefore arrives as an actual object and is rejected at line 79 (!is_object($v)); a serialized ARRAY arrives as an array and is rejected (!is_array($v)). So a single-layer serialized-object payload can never reach line 92 as a string. The only way to reach the sink with a serialized-object STRING is a double-serialized DB value (literal s:N:"O:8:...";) so core's single maybe_unserialize peels one layer and yields the inner object-string. This is contrived and requires storing an exact literal double-serialized string in a non-excluded, non-protected meta key.

2) Even if an object IS instantiated, the code only consumes is_array() results and extracts string scalars (lines 94-108); the object is discarded. Impact depends entirely on __wakeup/__destruct POP gadgets fired during unserialize().

3) No usable POP gadget chain exists in default WordPress core (historical ones were patched). RCE/file-write/SQLi would require a coincidental vulnerable third-party plugin, not present on a default install.

4) Trigger requires admin action (configuring and rendering/previewing a Posts generator against the attacker's post), and the attacker must be able to set arbitrary meta keys/values.

This is a real defense-in-depth hardening gap (should use unserialize($v, ['allowed_classes'=>false]) or maybe_unserialize with allowed_classes), but it is not a practically exploitable PHP Object Injection on a default/common install. Severity is overstated as medium; treat as low. Saving factors: WP core maybe_unserialize on retrieval + line 79 is_object/is_array guards + absence of a default-install gadget chain.

all_with_verdicts.title: Stored XSS via single-quote breakout in SlideEvent onclick handler
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: admin to configure the slideevent[...] link; effective payload can be supplied by a lower-privileged author/contributor through generator variables (post content/meta) substituted into the link
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: A layer link of the form 'slideevent[<argument>]' is parsed by Framework/Parser/Link::parse() (L28-33) which extracts <argument> (matches[2]) and passes it to SlideEvent::parse($argument). Before parsing, the link is run through Slide::fill() (AbstractItemFrontend::getLink L102-103, Slide::fill L513) which substitutes generator variables such as {title}/{content}/{meta...} drawn from WordPress posts (attacker-influenced content). SlideEvent::parse concatenates $argument directly into $attributes['onclick'] = "n2ss.trigger(this, '" . $argument . "', event);" with no escaping. The onclick attribute is later emitted by Html::renderAttributes() (Framework/View/Html.php L142) using encodeAttribute() which calls htmlspecialchars with ENT_COMPAT (L162-167) and deliberately does NOT encode single quotes. An attacker-controlled $argument containing a single quote, e.g. "');alert(document.cookie);//", breaks out of the JS string literal inside the rendered onclick="..." and executes arbitrary JavaScript on the front end for every visitor.

verdict.auth_required: admin (to configure the slideevent[...] link). The lower-privileged author/contributor path requires a non-default admin precondition that does not hold on a common install.
verdict.reasoning:
The raw technical chain is real and was verified: SlideEvent::parse (line 14) concatenates $argument into onclick="n2ss.trigger(this, '" . $argument . "', event);" with no escaping; Html::encodeAttribute (Html.php L162-167) uses htmlspecialchars with ENT_COMPAT | ENT_HTML5, which deliberately does NOT encode single quotes, while the attribute is emitted wrapped in double quotes (L142). A single quote in $argument therefore breaks out of the JS string literal. Slide::fill -> parseVariable (Slide.php L565) returns generator variable values verbatim with no escaping, so generator data is substituted raw. So the sink and encoder gap are genuine.

What defeats is_real on a default/common install is the attacker model, not a sanitizer. (1) Reaching this sink requires an admin to have explicitly configured a layer href as the slideevent[...] link type. The Link::parse javascript: block (L25) does not save it because this is the slideevent[...] branch, not a URL. (2) The admin-only case (admin types slideevent['); alert(1)//] directly) is admin self-XSS: Smart Slider 3 admins already configure raw JavaScript through numerous callback/JS fields, so an admin injecting JS into their own slider is not a privilege boundary crossing. (3) The lower-privileged (author/contributor) escalation the claim relies on requires the admin to have placed an attacker-influenced generator variable (e.g. {title}/{content}) INSIDE a slideevent[] argument (slideevent[{title}]). The normal use of slideevent[] is a static, admin-chosen event name; putting generator post data into the JS-event argument is a contrived, non-default configuration with no evidence of being common. Absent that specific admin setup, attacker-controlled post content never reaches this onclick sink.

Net: a genuine missing-escape code smell (ENT_COMPAT in a JS-context attribute plus unescaped concatenation), but no realistic exploit path for a non-admin attacker on a default/common install; the default-config impact reduces to admin self-XSS. Hence is_real=false, severity low.

all_with_verdicts.title: Stored XSS via unescaped Vimeo 'start' value concatenated into inline JavaScript
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: User able to edit slider/slide layer data (typically admin/editor), or attacker controlling dynamic generator variables substituted by Slide::fill()
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: ItemVimeo layer field 'start' (stored slide item value) -> ItemVimeoFrontend::render() reads $this->data->get('start','0') -> passed through $owner->fill() (Slide::fill(), which performs {variable}/{func()} template substitution and does NOT HTML/JS-escape, and can inject generator-sourced/external values) -> concatenated RAW (no json_encode, no quotes, no intval) as the final argument of the addScript() string: new _N2.FrontendItemVimeo(this, "id", "elementID", <toJSON>, <hasImage>, <start>); -> addScript() pushes the string into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks -> AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118/125) does implode($initCallbacks) and embeds it verbatim inside a JS function body: "initCallbacks":function(){ ...<start>... } -> emitted into the frontend page and executed by the browser. A 'start' value such as `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the function-call argument and runs arbitrary JS. Note the sibling YouTube item (ItemYouTubeFrontend.php line 119) handles the identical 'start' value safely by routing it only through json_encode via toJSON(), confirming this raw concatenation is a defect; the Number form-field constraint is client-side only and not enforced server-side at this sink.

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated user with the plugin's `smartslider_edit` capability (admin/editor) plus a valid CSRF token; the injected payload then executes for unauthenticated frontend visitors (stored XSS, not pure self-XSS).
verdict.reasoning:
The full exploit path holds end to end.

SINK (confirmed): ItemVimeoFrontend.php line 102 builds the init script by RAW concatenation of the last argument: `... , ' . $hasImage . ', ' . $owner->fill($this->data->get('start', '0')) . ');'` — no quotes, no json_encode, no intval. This string is pushed via addScript() into AbstractRenderable::$initCallbacks (AbstractRenderable.php:137), then AbstractSliderTypeFrontend::encodeJavaScriptProperties() (line 118 implode, line 125) embeds it VERBATIM inside `"initCallbacks":function(){ ... }` which is emitted to the page and executed. A value like `0);alert(document.cookie);//` breaks out of the call and runs arbitrary JS.

SOURCE / no sanitizer on any path:
- Save path: ControllerAjaxSlides::actionEdit() -> ModelSlides::save() -> convertSlideDataToDatabaseRow() (ModelSlides.php:658) stores `$row['slide'] = $slideData['layers']` (the full layer tree incl. `start`) as json_encode'd JSON with NO per-field sanitization. The `Number` form field (`min => 0`) in ItemVimeo::renderFields() is a client-side-only constraint, never enforced server-side at this sink.
- Slide::fill() (Slide.php:513) returns the value unchanged when no variables, or does {variable}/{func()} template substitution — neither path HTML/JS-escapes, so it cannot save the sink.
- The sink itself applies no escaping.

SIBLING PROOF: ItemYouTubeFrontend.php handles the identical `start` value safely by routing it only through toJSON()/json_encode (line 119, never raw-concatenated), confirming the Vimeo raw concatenation is a real defect, not intended design.

AUTH: actionEdit requires validateToken() (CSRF nonce) and validatePermission('smartslider_edit'). That gates exploitation to a privileged editing user, but the payload is stored and runs on the PUBLIC frontend for all visitors and any other admin viewing the slider — so it is genuine stored XSS reaching lower-privilege victims, not self-XSS. WP core defenses do not help: the value is placed in a JS execution context (not HTML), so wp_kses/esc_html are irrelevant, and the plugin applies none anyway.

Severity medium is correct: real and exploitable, but requires an authenticated slider-editing capability to plant the payload.
